scenario: Post-deadline limited strikes begin; Tehran Toll Booth remains active; Qatari LNG restrictions persist; AIS transits stay -95%

categories.label: Food & Agriculture
categories.supplyChainImpact: Middle East urea and DAP fertilizer exports transit Hormuz, supplying ~30% of Asia's planted-area inputs. Shipping delays of 10-14 days via Cape route raise input costs and threaten planting windows for Vietnam, Thailand, and Indonesia rice belts. Palm oil - Asia's dominant cooking oil - faces surging logistics costs as Malaysia and Indonesia divert vessels.

assets.name: Wheat
assets.narrative: Fertilizer cost pass-through remains acute as post-deadline strikes begin without restoring Gulf flows. Gulf urea remains severely dislocated and Egypt panic-buying continues. The risk is no longer a one-night binary event but an extended planting-season cost shock for Asia. Source: Barchart ZWK26.

assets.name: Corn
assets.narrative: Biofuel demand remains elevated as Brent pushes toward a new post-deadline high. Indonesian ethanol demand still competes with feed use, while fertilizer and freight costs stay elevated. Tehran Toll Booth economics and prolonged disruption keep the next planting cycle under pressure.

assets.name: Soybeans
assets.narrative: Bunker costs rise again as Brent reprices higher after the missed deadline. Indonesian and Vietnamese crush margins remain pressured by freight rather than direct Gulf supply loss, which keeps soy upside below energy and fertilizer but leaves the freight overhang intact.

assets.name: Palm Oil (CPO)
assets.narrative: Crude oil correlation strengthens again as Brent reprices after post-deadline strikes. Biodiesel feedstock demand remains elevated, Indonesia's B40 mandate is intact, and fertilizer disruption still threatens the next yield cycle. Source: Bursa Malaysia BMD.

assets.name: Rice (Thai 25% FOB)
assets.narrative: ASEAN food security pressure remains high: fertilizer shortfall still threatens Vietnam and Thailand rice belts, while distressed freight keeps import costs elevated. The Philippines' bilateral Hormuz deal helps routing but not fertilizer availability. No post-deadline relief is visible.

categories.label: Petrochemicals & Plastics
categories.supplyChainImpact: SABIC (Saudi), QAPCO (Qatar), and OCI (Oman) are among the world's largest petrochemical exporters - all ship via Hormuz. SE Asian crackers in Singapore, Thailand, and Malaysia rely on naphtha feedstock sourced from Gulf condensate. Downstream plastics, packaging, and agrochemicals face double squeeze: higher feedstock costs and logistics disruption.

assets.name: Naphtha (CFR Japan)
assets.narrative: Gulf condensate-derived naphtha reprices sharply higher as post-deadline strikes begin. Tehran Toll Booth remains active, Qatar force majeure is unresolved, and no near-term supply restoration path is visible. Jurong Island and Map Ta Phut crackers remain under feedstock pressure. Source: SGX NJF1 futures.

assets.name: Ethylene (CFR NE Asia)
assets.narrative: Ethylene remains downstream of the renewed naphtha spike. SABIC and QAPCO exports are still bottlenecked, and the latest escalation extends the pass-through into Southeast Asian plastics chains. Agricultural film and irrigation pipe lead times remain stretched. Source: ICE/ICIS CFR NE Asia.

assets.name: Methanol (CFR China)
assets.narrative: Iran remains one of the world's largest methanol exporters, and post-deadline strikes further delay any restart path. Qatar force majeure is still in effect and China MTO complexes remain under feedstock squeeze. Partial offset from US and Chinese domestic supply is no longer enough to keep the market calm. Source: Investing.com CFR China futures.

categories.label: Fertilizers
categories.supplyChainImpact: The Strait of Hormuz is the critical export artery for Middle East fertilizers: Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman, and UAE together account for ~18% of global urea supply and ~12% of DAP. QatarEnergy (Qafco) - world's largest urea exporter - declared force majeure on Mar 2. Iran is also a significant potash and urea producer. Asian planting windows create an acute near-term demand cliff.

assets.name: Urea (FOB Middle East)
assets.narrative: Qafco force majeure remains in effect and Saudi urea capacity is still constrained. Post-deadline strikes remove any residual near-term restart hope while Tehran Toll Booth economics keep Hormuz access structurally uncertain. Egypt and India continue panic-buying at large premiums. Source: DTN, Argus Fertilizers.

assets.name: DAP (FOB US Gulf)
assets.narrative: Saudi Ma'aden DAP shipping remains disrupted and Toll Booth transit costs still add a delivered premium. The post-deadline escalation raises the probability of longer Saudi export dislocation. US Gulf supply is still filling the gap at premium freight. Wet-season planting pressure in the Philippines and Vietnam persists.

assets.name: Potash (MOP CFR SE Asia)
assets.narrative: Potash is less directly exposed to Hormuz than urea or DAP, but elevated freight costs continue to add indirect pressure. The post-deadline escalation keeps war-risk freight premiums elevated. Palm oil plantations remain the key Southeast Asian transmission channel.

categories.label: Shipping & Freight
categories.supplyChainImpact: P&I club war risk coverage withdrawal (effective March 5) triggered mass tanker re-routing via Cape of Good Hope, adding 10-14 transit days and absorbing ~8% of global VLCC fleet capacity. Container lines have activated war risk surcharges and re-routed services, extending Asia-Europe transit times. Bunker fuel costs elevated by crude spike compound freight economics.

assets.name: Baltic Dry Index
assets.narrative: Cape rerouting continues to absorb dry-bulk capacity as commodity flows avoid the Gulf. The missed deadline confirms there is no near-term Hormuz routing recovery, so vessels remain committed to longer patterns through at least Q2. Source: Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News.

assets.name: VLCC AG-Asia Rate
assets.narrative: Tehran Toll Booth remains active and VLCC owners still face a dual-pricing structure. The missed deadline converts pre-deadline risk premium into active disruption premium. P&I restoration remains impossible without a political settlement, and any phase-two strike on export infrastructure would push rates materially higher again. Source: Seatrade Maritime, Lloyd's List.

assets.name: Container Freight (SCFI)
assets.narrative: Asia-Europe container rates remain elevated after the missed deadline; there is still no Suez normalization path. Philippines bilateral relief remains too narrow to change the wider freight picture, and Southeast Asian manufacturers continue to face extended lead times. Source: Shanghai Shipping Exchange.
